perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby affirms that it is not prohibited by the Office of Export Administration for the U.S. Department of Commerce from receiving technical information, know-how, data or other information and each party agrees not to export such information, or products incorporating it, to any prohibited country.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1. This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties and their respective successors and assigns. This Agreement shall not be assignable by either party without the written consent of the other party, and any purported assignment not permitted hereunder shall be void. Each party hereby affirms that it is not prohibited by the Office of Export Administration for the U.S. Department of Commerce from receiving technical information, know-how, data or other information and each party agrees not to export such information, or products incorporating it, to any prohibited country. This document constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and shall supersede all previous communications, representations, understandings and agreements, whether oral or written, between the parties or any official or representative thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The clause about export restrictions is moved into a section concerning agreement assignment and entire agreement, where it doesn't logically belong. This obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the export restriction affirmation to a section on agreement assignment buries a critical compliance statement, potentially leading to violations of EAR due to insufficient emphasis and clarity. 15 CFR § 732.1 emphasizes understanding and complying with export control regulations, a goal undermined by obscuring the affirmation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of moving the export restriction affirmation into a section concerning agreement assignment undermines the emphasis and clarity required for compliance, as suggested in the Law Explanation. This action effectively buries a critical compliance statement, potentially leading to violations of EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates that understanding the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774 is crucial. By obscuring the export restriction affirmation, the modified text contradicts the intent of ensuring compliance with export control regulations, thus creating a legal contradiction according to the definition provided for Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All confidential information shall remain the property of the disclosing party. Further, the receiving party agrees to return to the disclosing party, upon request, any information disclosed in any tangible form, all copies thereof, containing any of the confidential information referred to in paragraph number 1 above.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby affirms that it is not prohibited by the Office of Export Administration for the U.S. Department of Commerce from receiving technical information, know-how, data or other information and each party agrees not to export such information, or products incorporating it, to any prohibited country. Further, the receiving party agrees to return to the disclosing party, upon request, any information disclosed in any tangible form, all copies thereof, containing any of the confidential information referred to in paragraph number 1 above.
perturbation.explanation: The statement about ownership of confidential information is replaced with the export restriction statement, hiding it within another section. This diminishes the importance of clearly establishing ownership of confidential data.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the clause that establishes ownership of the confidential information, the contract weakens the protection of Trade Secrets, regulated by 18 U.S. Code § 1839. This law defines and protects trade secrets, and clear ownership is essential for enforcing these protections.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly established ownership of confidential information, which is a vital aspect of trade secret protection. The modified contract replaces this clause with export restrictions, creating a structural flaw by obscuring or removing the clear statement of ownership. According to the Law Explanation, removing this clause weakens the protection of trade secrets, regulated by 18 U.S. Code § 1839, where clear ownership is essential for enforcement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 contains the text "18 USC 1839 : Definitions Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1839. Definitions". The changed text contradicts the establishment of ownership of trade secrets, a vital aspect for enforceability under 18 U.S. Code § 1839.
